title: Congress of Vienna concludes
summary: On 9 June 1815, nine days before the guns opened at Waterloo, the plenipotentiaries of two hundred European states signed the Final Act of the Congress of Vienna. Convened in September 1814 in the Habsburg capital under the chairmanship of Prince Klemens von Metternich, the congress redrew the map of a continent shattered by twenty-three years of revolutionary and Napoleonic war. It restored old dynasties, invented new buffer states, condemned the slave trade, guaranteed river navigation and Swiss neutrality, and installed a Concert of Europe that would keep the great powers from general war for almost a century.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
By the spring of 1814 Napoleon's empire had collapsed. The allied armies of Russia, Prussia, Austria and the smaller German states had marched through Champagne, entered Paris on 31 March, and forced the emperor's abdication at Fontainebleau on 6 April. The First Treaty of Paris, signed on 30 May, restored the Bourbon Louis XVIII to the French throne, reduced France to its frontiers of 1792, and provided that a general congress in Vienna would settle the outstanding questions of the war within two months. It took ten.

The scale of what had to be settled was without precedent. The Holy Roman Empire had been dissolved in 1806 and its three hundred territories consolidated by Napoleon into thirty-nine states; the Duchy of Warsaw carved from Prussian, Austrian and Russian Poland needed to be redistributed; the Kingdom of the Netherlands had to be reconstituted from the former Austrian Netherlands and the Dutch Republic; Italy required a fresh dynastic order after the fall of the Bonapartist kingdoms of Naples and Italy; and every colonial possession that Britain had seized during the war, from the Cape of Good Hope to Ceylon and Guiana, had to be assigned.

Delegations began to arrive in Vienna in September 1814. The four allied powers meant to settle the essentials among themselves. Austria was represented by Prince Klemens von Metternich, the thirty-nine-year-old foreign minister whose smooth conservatism would give his name to the age; Russia by the mystical, unpredictable Tsar Alexander I in person, with Karl Nesselrode at his elbow; Prussia by the chancellor Karl August von Hardenberg and the philologist Wilhelm von Humboldt; and Britain by the foreign secretary Viscount Castlereagh, later relieved by the Duke of Wellington after his return from the embassy in Paris. To their consternation the defeated French sent Charles-Maurice de Talleyrand, the limping ex-bishop of Autun who had served the ancien regime, the Republic, the Empire and the Restoration in turn, and who now insisted on the principle of legitimacy as his ticket into the inner circle. Rounding out the cast were the Portuguese, Spanish and Swedish envoys who eventually formed the Committee of Eight, the papal legate Cardinal Ercole Consalvi, and a swarm of representatives from smaller German and Italian courts. The Emperor Francis I of Austria hosted a season of hunts, balls and receptions so extravagant that the Prince de Ligne quipped, 'le congres ne marche pas, il danse'.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The first crisis came in October and November 1814 over Poland and Saxony. Tsar Alexander demanded the whole Duchy of Warsaw as a Russian-controlled Kingdom of Poland; Prussia was to be compensated with the entire kingdom of Saxony, whose ruler had unwisely stayed loyal to Napoleon. Metternich and Castlereagh refused to accept a Russian frontier so far to the west or the extinction of an old German dynasty. Talleyrand, exploiting the deadlock, brought France to the centre of the discussion. On 3 January 1815 Britain, Austria and France signed a secret defensive alliance against Russia and Prussia. The tsar, warned of the treaty, backed down. Russia received a smaller Congress Kingdom of Poland; Prussia was given some sixty per cent of Saxony, the Rhineland and Swedish Pomerania; the King of Saxony kept his throne over the remainder.

The rest of the settlement followed in the winter and spring of 1815. Austria surrendered its old Belgian provinces but received Lombardy and Venetia in northern Italy, together with Salzburg, the Tyrol, and its old possessions in Dalmatia. The Kingdom of the Netherlands, joining the northern Dutch provinces to Belgium and Luxembourg, was created as a barrier state against any French resurgence, and William of Orange took the throne. Piedmont-Sardinia regained Savoy and Nice and was enlarged by the annexation of Genoa. The Papal States, Tuscany, Parma, Modena and the Two Sicilies were restored, Marie Louise, Napoleon's second wife, receiving Parma for life. The thirty-nine surviving German states were bound into a loose German Confederation under Austrian presidency, meeting at Frankfurt.

On 1 March 1815 the news reached Vienna that Napoleon had escaped from Elba. The Eight Powers immediately declared him an outlaw and formed the Seventh Coalition, but the diplomatic work continued through the Hundred Days. The Final Act of the Congress, a single treaty of one hundred and twenty-one articles binding together all the separate agreements, was signed on 9 June 1815 by the representatives of Austria, Britain, France, Portugal, Prussia, Russia, Spain and Sweden and later adhered to by thirty-five other states. Nine days later Wellington and Blucher defeated the returned emperor at Waterloo. A second Treaty of Paris in November 1815 rolled the French border back to 1790, imposed an indemnity of seven hundred million francs and an army of occupation, and completed the Vienna settlement.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The territorial map produced at Vienna lasted, in its main lines, until 1859 in Italy, 1866 in Germany and, in the case of the Netherlands settlement, until Belgian independence in 1830 and 1839. The Concert of Europe, an informal system by which the five great powers met in periodic congresses to manage crises, held the continent together with remarkable success. Congresses at Aix-la-Chapelle in 1818, Troppau in 1820, Laibach in 1821 and Verona in 1822 handled the readmission of France, the Neapolitan and Spanish revolutions, and the Greek war of independence. Direct war between the great powers of Europe would not resume until the Crimean War of 1853 to 1856, and after that not until 1866. Between 1815 and 1914 the continent enjoyed the longest general peace in its recorded history.

On 26 September 1815 Tsar Alexander persuaded the emperor of Austria and the king of Prussia to sign the Holy Alliance, a vague personal engagement of the three Christian monarchs to govern according to Christian principles. Castlereagh dismissed it as a piece of sublime mysticism and nonsense, but the Holy Alliance became, in the minds of European liberals, the symbol of the reactionary post-1815 order. The Quadruple Alliance signed on 20 November 1815 among Britain, Russia, Austria and Prussia provided the sharper diplomatic teeth.

The settlement also produced advances that later generations recognised as genuinely progressive. Article 118 committed the powers to work for the universal abolition of the transatlantic slave trade, following a British campaign led by William Wilberforce and Castlereagh himself. Free navigation of the Rhine, Danube, Meuse, Elbe and other international rivers was guaranteed. The perpetual neutrality of Switzerland was formally recognised on 20 November 1815, a status the country has kept ever since. And the format itself, plenipotentiaries of many states gathered simultaneously in one capital, provided the template for every subsequent international conference from Paris in 1919 to San Francisco in 1945.

The critics were nonetheless loud. Liberal and nationalist opinion across Europe denounced the congress for handing Poles to Russians, Belgians to Dutch, Italians to Austrians, and Germans to a patchwork of restored princes without regard to language or aspiration. The revolutions of 1830 and 1848 were in part revolts against the Vienna order. Yet Metternich's system had preserved the general peace of Europe long enough for railways, cotton mills, popular literacy and mass politics to transform the ground beneath it.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
For most of the nineteenth century the Congress of Vienna was remembered as the archetype of aristocratic reaction, a diplomacy of princes conducted over the heads of nations. Twentieth-century historians, chastened by the catastrophic failure of the 1919 Paris settlement to prevent a second world war within a generation, took a kinder view. Henry Kissinger's Harvard doctoral thesis, published in 1957 as A World Restored, treated Metternich and Castlereagh as pioneers of a workable international order built on shared conservative interests, self-restraint by the victors, and the reintegration of the defeated power. Paul Schroeder's Transformation of European Politics 1763 to 1848, published in 1994, went further and argued that Vienna produced a fundamental change from the eighteenth-century balance-of-power system to a genuine political equilibrium in which the great states policed themselves for their common security.

The congress also left an enduring diplomatic vocabulary. Terms such as great power, sphere of influence, plenipotentiary and concert of nations were codified or popularised in the meetings on the Ballhausplatz. The rules on rank and precedence among diplomats agreed on 19 March 1815 remained the basis of the international diplomatic system until the Vienna Convention of 1961. The federal structure of the German Confederation shaped the debates that would culminate in Bismarck's unification of Germany in 1871. The Congress Kingdom of Poland, though repeatedly suppressed, kept the Polish national idea alive until the restoration of independence in 1918. And the memory of the great ball season under Emperor Francis, immortalised in Prince Metternich's memoirs and later in operetta, made Vienna itself, for the rest of the nineteenth century, the emblematic capital of European high politics.

worldContext.text: The Spanish American wars of independence were in full career. Simon Bolivar, in exile in Jamaica in September 1815, wrote the Jamaica Letter setting out his vision of independent Spanish American republics; Jose de San Martin was assembling in Mendoza the Army of the Andes that would liberate Chile in 1817. The War of 1812 between Britain and the United States had ended in December 1814 with the Treaty of Ghent; Andrew Jackson's victory at New Orleans on 8 January 1815 was fought after the peace was signed but became the founding legend of American nationalism.

The Qing empire under the Jiaqing Emperor was managing the aftermath of the White Lotus Rebellion, which had exhausted the imperial treasury, and dealing with growing British East India Company pressure to open the China trade beyond Canton. In September 1816 the British ambassador Lord Amherst would be turned away from Beijing after refusing to kowtow. In Japan the Tokugawa shogunate under Ienari continued the sakoku policy that limited foreign contact to a handful of Dutch and Chinese ships at Nagasaki, though the growing appetite for Western learning through rangaku was creating a new class of scholars in Osaka and Edo.

In India the British East India Company under Governor-General Lord Moira was fighting the Anglo-Nepalese War of 1814 to 1816, which ended with the Treaty of Sugauli establishing the modern frontiers of Nepal and beginning Gurkha recruitment into the Company's armies. The Maratha states, defeated in the Second Anglo-Maratha War of 1803 to 1805, were preparing for the Third Anglo-Maratha War that would end their independent existence in 1818. In Punjab, Ranjit Singh's Sikh kingdom continued to expand into Kashmir and the trans-Indus territories.

The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Mahmud II was preoccupied with the First Saudi State, whose Wahhabi warriors had captured Mecca and Medina; an Egyptian expeditionary force under Muhammad Ali's son Ibrahim Pasha was pushing into the Arabian peninsula and would sack the Saudi capital of Diriyah in 1818. In Persia the Qajar shah Fath-Ali had just lost his first war with Russia, sealed by the Treaty of Gulistan in October 1813, ceding Georgia, Dagestan and most of what is now Azerbaijan.

The Sokoto Caliphate proclaimed in 1809 by Usman dan Fodio dominated the Hausa states of West Africa; his son Muhammad Bello, who succeeded him in 1817, would extend the caliphate to its greatest extent. In southern Africa the Zulu kingdom under Shaka was in the first years of its rise; his military reforms and campaigns would from about 1816 unleash the mfecane migrations across the interior. Along the Barbary coast the American Second Barbary War of 1815 ended North African corsair raiding on United States shipping.

The colony of New South Wales under Governor Lachlan Macquarie was moving beyond its convict origins; the crossing of the Blue Mountains by Gregory Blaxland, William Lawson and William Wentworth in 1813 had opened the interior of Australia to European settlement, and Macquarie was laying out a program of public works and towns. In New Zealand the Church Missionary Society under Samuel Marsden established the first permanent Christian mission at the Bay of Islands in December 1814, beginning the long transformation of Maori society by literacy and Christianity.

figures.name: Klemens von Metternich
figures.role: Austrian foreign minister and host of the Congress; principal architect of the post-1815 conservative order

figures.name: Viscount Castlereagh
figures.role: British foreign secretary who led the British delegation until February 1815 and negotiated the Quadruple Alliance

figures.name: Charles-Maurice de Talleyrand
figures.role: Foreign minister of the restored French monarchy who manoeuvred France back into the ranks of the great powers

figures.name: Tsar Alexander I
figures.role: Emperor of Russia who attended the congress in person and proposed the Holy Alliance

figures.name: Karl August von Hardenberg
figures.role: Prussian chancellor who secured his kingdom's Rhenish and Saxon territorial gains

figures.name: Duke of Wellington
figures.role: British ambassador and, from February 1815, chief British plenipotentiary; commander at Waterloo the following June

images.caption: The plenipotentiaries of the Congress of Vienna, engraving after Jean-Baptiste Isabey, 1815. Metternich stands at left, Talleyrand is seated at right; Wellington is at the far left.
images.credit: Jean-Baptiste Isabey (1767 to 1855), via Wikimedia Commons

sources.title: The Final Act of the Congress of Vienna
